title: Detect Start of Cycle in Linked List

content.problemStatement:
You are given a singly linked list with NNN nodes. Each node contains an integer value.

Your task is to find the **index of the node where a cycle begins**, if a cycle exists. If the linked list has no cycle, print −1-1−1.

A cycle occurs when a node's next pointer points to a **previous node** or **itself** in the list. The pospospos is the 0-based index of the node that the last node points to.

Function Declaration**Function Name**detectCycledetectCycledetectCycle — This function determines whether a cycle exists in a singly linked list and returns the index of the node where the cycle begins.

Parametersheadheadhead: A pointer to the **first node** of the singly linked list.


May be NULLNULLNULL if the list is empty.
Each node contains an integer value and a pointer to the next node.
Return ValueReturns an integer representing the **0-based index of the node** where the cycle starts.


If **no cycle** is present in the linked list, returns −1-1−1.
`The input and output formats provided below are only for testing with custom inputs.`

Constraints:
0≤N≤1040 \le N \le 10^{4}0≤N≤104
−105≤Node.val≤105-10^{5} \le \text{Node.val} \le 10^{5}−105≤Node.val≤105
−1≤pos<N-1 \le \text{pos} < N−1≤pos<N

Input Format

The first line contains an integer NNN — the number of nodes in the linked list.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers — the values of the nodes.
The third line contains an integer pospospos — the index that the last node connects to (−1-1−1 if no cycle).


Output Format

Print a single integer — the 0-based index of the node where the cycle begins.
If there is no cycle, print −1-1−1.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains three lines: the first line contains an integer N, the number of nodes in the linked list; the second line contains N space-separated integers, the values of the nodes; the third line contains an integer pos, the index that the last node connects to (−1 if no cycle).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single integer — the 0-based index of the node where the cycle begins. If there is no cycle, print −1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The list has 7 nodes. The last node (value 77) points to index 4 (value 55). Floyd's algorithm finds the cycle and the start index is 4.
The last node (15) points to index 1 (value 6). The cycle begins at index 1.
No node points back, so there is no cycle.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using Floyd's cycle detection algorithm, also known as the 'tortoise and the hare' algorithm. This algorithm uses two pointers that move at different speeds through the linked list. If there is a cycle, these two pointers will eventually meet at some node. Once a cycle is detected, we can find the start of the cycle by moving one of the pointers back to the beginning of the linked list and keeping the other pointer at the meeting point. Then, we move both pointers one step at a time, and the point where they meet again is the start of the cycle.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T*N) total, where N is the number of nodes in the linked list.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the linked list, where N is the number of nodes in the linked list.
